name: Taweret
description: Taweret, also spelled Taurt, Thoueris, or Toeris, is a protective ancient Egyptian deity of childbirth and fertility. Her syncretic image, a combination of hippopotamus, lion, and crocodile, reflects the dangerous and unpredictable nature of childbirth itself. As a goddess, Taweret was not associated with royalty or elite cults, but rather was a popular household deity, venerated for her ability to ward off evil spirits and protect women during pregnancy and labor. She is often depicted standing upright, possessing the head of a hippopotamus, the tail of a crocodile, and the paws of a lion, a visually striking representation of her power. Amulets and figurines of Taweret were commonly found in homes, reflecting her significance in everyday life. While not typically featured in grand narratives of the Egyptian pantheon, her enduring presence in domestic contexts underscores her importance in ensuring the safe arrival of new life and the well-being of mothers.
subtitle: The Great One

keyMyths.title: Taweret's Protective Role
keyMyths.description: Taweret's primary mythic role is as a protector, particularly of pregnant women and newborn children. She was believed to ward off malevolent spirits and demons, such as Ammit, who threatened the vulnerable. Her fierce appearance was intended to frighten away evil forces, ensuring a safe delivery and the healthy development of the child. This protective function underscores her importance in the daily lives of ancient Egyptians, particularly women.
keyMyths.source: Pinch, Geraldine. *Magic in Ancient Egypt*. University of Texas Press, 2006.

keyMyths.title: Taweret and the Celestial Waters
keyMyths.description: As 'Mistress of Pure Water', Taweret has connections to the life-giving waters of the Nile and the celestial waters believed to exist beyond the sky. This association links her to the regenerative powers of water and its essential role in sustaining life. Some scholars suggest a connection between Taweret and the Milky Way, seen as a celestial river from which life springs, further emphasizing her role as a source of creation and nourishment.
keyMyths.source: Wilkinson, Richard H. *The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt*. Thames & Hudson, 2003.

keyMyths.title: Taweret and Bes
keyMyths.description: Taweret is frequently associated with the dwarf god Bes, another protective deity of childbirth and the home. Together, they formed a powerful duo, offering comprehensive protection against evil spirits and ensuring the well-being of families. Their combined imagery, often found on amulets and household objects, reflects the pervasive belief in the need for divine intervention to safeguard the vulnerable members of society.
keyMyths.source: Frankfort, Henri. *Ancient Egyptian Religion*. Columbia University Press, 1948.

sources.author: Wilkinson, Richard H.
sources.work: The Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Egyptian deities, including Taweret, with detailed descriptions of her iconography and attributes.
